content.full_text:
air Political Practices CommissioIL 
BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance/Administration 
(916) 322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
June IS, 1976 
Ms. Carolyn L. Kemmler 
Public Advisor 
Energy Resources Conservation 
and Development Commission 
1111 Howe Avenue 
Sacramento, California 
95825 
Dear Ms. Kenunler: 
Enforcement/Conflict of Interest 
322-6441 
Your letter of June 3, 1976, to Mr. Lowenstein, has been 
referred to me for a response. 
You inquire as to whether a 
financial interest in a mutual fund would be an "investment" 
within the meaning of §2402(n) of the proposed State Energy 
Resources Conservation and Development Commission Conflict 
of Interest Code. 
As the definition in that Section simply 
tracks the definition of "investment" contained in Government 
Code §82034, I interpret your question to be whether a finan-
cial interest in a mutual fund would be an "investment" within 
the meaning of §82034 of the Government Code. 
§82034 provides, in pertinent part, that: 
"'Investment' means any financial interest in or security 
issued by a business entity, including but not limited to 
common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, 
debt instruments and any partnership or other ownership 
interest .... " 
"Business entity" as defined in Government Code §82005, 
means: 
" .•.. any organization or enterprise operated for profit, 
including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, 
firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation 
or association." 
If a mutual fund is a "business entity", then a "financial 
interest" in a mutual fund would clearly be within the language 
of the definition of "investments". 


Ms. Carolyn L. Kennler 
June 15, 1976 
Page Two 
Further, it is my understanding that mutual funds issue 
either shares of co~~on stock in the fund or a certificate of 
interest in the assets of the fund to their investors, and 
this stock or certificate interest in the assets gives the 
investor the "right" to vote the percentage interest of 
assets held. 
An investment in the form of common stock is 
clearly within the above definition of investment, while a 
certificate of interest would seem to evidence an ownership 
interest in the fund, if not be the equivalent of a security, 
and as such, would bring the holder of such an interest within 
the definition of "investment" as the definition includes 
any "security issued by a business entity, including but not 
limited to 
rights ... and any ... other ownership interest". 
Thus, the answer to your first question is that a finan-
cial interest in a mutual fund is an "investment" within the 
meaning of Government Code §82034. 
The second question in your letter asks whether the 
deferred compensation of an employee, invested in either the 
T. Rowe Price Growth Fund, Inc., or the Rowe Price New Income 
Fund, Inc., does or could result in a conflict of interest 
under the Political Reform Act of 1974. 
As both of these 
funds are mutual funds, an investment in either would, as 
the response to your first question indicates, be an "invest-
ment" within the meaning of Government Code §82034. 
Accord-
ingly, if an employee has such an investment, and it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the investment could be affected 
materially by a decision made or participated in by the 
employee, and the effect is distinguishable from the effect 
on the public generally, the employee would have a conflict 
of interest and would, under the provisions of Government 
Code §87302(c) and §2413 of the proposed Conflict of Interest 
Code of the State Energy Resources Conservation and Develop-
ment Commission, be required to disqualify himself or herself. 
Whether there is, in fact, a conflict of interest for 
any given employee who holds such an investment can only be 
determined by a review of the duties and responsibilities of 
the employee. 
I trust that these informal comments will be of some 
assistance to you. 
Please let me know if I can be of further 
help to you. 
Sincerely, 
/"> 
? 
/ 
/' ;.:/7'/-~"L-
Edg'ar A. 
Staff Attorn 
Conflicts of Interest 
Division 
(916) 322-6444 


STATE OF 'CALIFORNIA-THE RESOURCES AGENCY 
EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor 
= 
ENERGY RESOURCES CONSERVATION 
AND DEVelOPMENT COMMISSION 
11 t 1 HOWE AVENUE 
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95825 
Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practice Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
June 3, 1976 
Re: 
Deferred Compensation for State 
Employees--Possible Conflict of Interest 
for Energy Commission Employees 
Dear Chairman Lowenstein: 
It has corne to my attention that section 2402(n) 
of the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development 
Commission Conflict of Interest Code (reference, Government 
Code, section 82033), does not specifically mention mutual 
funds as an "investment" within the meaning of the sub-
section. 
Despite the omission, I believe it may be con-
cluded that the intent of the subsection is to include mutual 
funds as an investment. 
As you may know, the state offers a deferred 
compensation program to its employees. 
Alternative invest-
ments under this plan include both the T. Rowe Price Growth 
Stock Fund, Inc. and the Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc. 
My question is whether the investment by an employee of the 
State Energy Commission in either of these funds under the 
deferred compensation plan 
does or could result in a conflict 
of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974. 
An informal opinion would be appreciated at your 
early convenience. 
Sincerely, 
ser 
:mb

sections.question: You inquire as to whether a financial interest in a mutual fund would be an "investment" within the meaning of §2402(n) of the proposed State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission Conflict of Interest Code. [Second question:] Whether the deferred compensation of an employee, invested in either the T. Rowe Price Growth Fund, Inc., or the Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc., does or could result in a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
sections.conclusion: Thus, the answer to your first question is that a financial interest in a mutual fund is an "investment" within the meaning of Government Code §82034. [Regarding the second question:] Accordingly, if an employee has such an investment, and it is reasonably foreseeable that the investment could be affected materially by a decision made or participated in by the employee, and the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, the employee would have a conflict of interest and would, under the provisions of Government Code §87302(c) and §2413 of the proposed Conflict of Interest Code of the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission, be required to disqualify himself or herself.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a financial interest in a mutual fund constitute an "investment" under Government Code §82034, and would deferred compensation invested in specific mutual funds result in a conflict of interest for Energy Commission employees?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mutual fund investments qualify as "investments" under Government Code §82034. A conflict of interest arises when an employee's decision could materially affect the value of their mutual fund investment in a way distinguishable from effects on the general public, requiring disqualification in such cases.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (June 3, 1976 letter from Ms. Kemmler/Commission) and FPPC response (June 15, 1976 letter from Edgar A., Staff Attorney). Formal question and conclusion sections present. Document is opinion/advice letter from FPPC to outside agency regarding conflict of interest applicability.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You inquire as to whether a financial interest in a mutual fund would be an "investment" within the meaning of §2402(n) of the proposed State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission Conflict of Interest Code. [Second question:] Whether the deferred compensation of an employee, invested in either the T. Rowe Price Growth Fund, Inc., or the Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc., does or could result in a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974.

CONCLUSION: Thus, the answer to your first question is that a financial interest in a mutual fund is an "investment" within the meaning of Government Code §82034. [Regarding the second question:] Accordingly, if an employee has such an investment, and it is reasonably foreseeable that the investment could be affected materially by a decision made or participated in by the employee, and the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, the employee would have a conflict of interest and would, under the provisions of Government Code §87302(c) and §2413 of the proposed Conflict of Interest Code of the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission, be required to disqualify himself or herself.

embedding.summary: FPPC staff attorney Edgar A. responds to inquiry from the Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission regarding whether mutual funds constitute reportable investments and whether employee deferred compensation in specific mutual funds creates conflicts of interest. The letter concludes that mutual funds are investments under state law and that conflicts arise when an employee's decisions could materially affect the investment's value.
